title: Lebanon in the Shadow of the U.S.-Iran MOU: Risks and Opportunities
gist: CSIS's Paul Salem argues the U.S.-Iran MOU's Lucerne talks -- which explicitly include Lebanon and floated a U.S.-Iran-Lebanon 'deconfliction cell' -- offer a rare but fragile opening to secure Israeli withdrawal and Hezbollah's disarmament under Lebanese Armed Forces control, but built-in ambiguities (Israel's occupation conditionality, Hezbollah's 'resistance' framing, and U.S. tolerance of Israeli strikes) could instead entrench a decades-long stalemate resembling 1982-2000.

insights.claim: Lebanon's three core strategic objectives in the current ceasefire negotiations are an Israeli withdrawal from Lebanese territory, a strengthened Lebanese state, and bringing Hezbollah's arms under the state's exclusive control -- and the war has pushed the country further from all three.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: Sets the three concrete benchmarks to track to gauge whether the ceasefire process is actually working for Lebanon versus merely freezing the conflict.

insights.claim: The U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding's first clause explicitly includes Lebanon within the ceasefire framework and commits both sides to respecting Lebanon's sovereignty and territorial integrity.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: Confirms Lebanon is a formal, not incidental, party to the U.S.-Iran deconfliction process -- its fate is now legally tied to the broader U.S.-Iran diplomatic track underway in Lucerne.
insights.locator: The very first clause includes Lebanon

insights.claim: A 'deconfliction cell' involving the United States, Iran, and Lebanon has been discussed in the Lucerne, Switzerland talks to oversee the cessation of military operations, but Israel is not currently part of that cell.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: Israel's exclusion from the deconfliction cell is a structural gap that could undermine enforcement -- a specific mechanism to watch for whether and when Israel joins.

themes.theme: Structural obstacles to a durable ceasefire

insights.claim: Israel has said it will respect a ceasefire only if Hezbollah does the same, but has also made clear it will not withdraw from occupied South Lebanon as long as it perceives a Hezbollah threat -- creating a circular precondition that could stall withdrawal indefinitely.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: Identifies the core deadlock mechanism: Israeli withdrawal is conditioned on Hezbollah's disarmament, but disarmament talks are themselves contingent on Israeli withdrawal -- a chicken-and-egg risk for any ceasefire timeline.
insights.quote: it does not intend to withdraw from occupied territory so long as it believes the Hezbollah threat persists
insights.locator: Israel has indicated that it is prepared to respect

insights.claim: Even if Hezbollah suspends attacks on Israeli soil, it continues to frame military action against Israeli forces inside Lebanese territory as part of its 'resistance' mission, which builds in a persistent source of instability even during reduced-violence periods.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: Means a 'ceasefire' in Lebanon is likely to remain a low-intensity, contested arrangement rather than a clean stop -- relevant for pricing in continued episodic escalation risk.
insights.locator: Hezbollah may be willing to suspend attacks

insights.claim: The U.S. has repeatedly supported ceasefire arrangements in Lebanon while simultaneously endorsing Israel's right to strike targets it deems an imminent threat, an ambiguity the author says has weakened previous ceasefires and could undermine this one.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: Flags a specific, recurring policy inconsistency in U.S. Lebanon diplomacy that has a track record of eroding prior truces -- a base-rate reason to discount ceasefire durability.
insights.locator: the United States has repeatedly supported ceasefire

insights.claim: The author recommends a phased Israeli withdrawal from Lebanese territory in which the Lebanese Armed Forces, backed by U.S. Central Command and partner militaries, assume full security responsibility in the south and ensure the absence of Hezbollah fighters and facilities.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: Names the specific security-handoff mechanism (LAF plus CENTCOM backing) that would need to materialize for a durable de-escalation -- a concrete marker to track in coming months.
insights.locator: the parties—supported by sustained U.S. engagement

insights.claim: Any broader U.S.-Iran understanding should be paired with withdrawal of IRGC personnel and infrastructure from Lebanon and a credible process restoring the Lebanese state's monopoly on the use of force, according to the author.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: Sets the author's explicit litmus test for whether the U.S.-Iran deal genuinely resolves Lebanon's crisis or merely relocates it -- IRGC withdrawal is the tell.
insights.locator: An Israeli withdrawal should be accompanied

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia, Turkey, Qatar, Egypt, and Pakistan have all become active mediators or facilitators in Lebanon's postwar diplomacy, sharing an interest in a more stable Levant.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: Identifies the regional coalition whose diplomatic engagement could add leverage to push Hezbollah's integration forward, beyond just U.S.-Iran bilateral talks.
insights.locator: Several regional powers have also become active

insights.claim: The author draws on the IRA in Northern Ireland and the FARC in Colombia as precedents showing that disarmament and state consolidation rarely succeed through coercion alone, arguing Lebanon needs a genuine political dialogue track with Hezbollah alongside pressure.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: Offers a template (negotiated demobilization, not just military pressure) for how Hezbollah's disarmament could realistically play out -- relevant for timeline expectations on any 'Hezbollah disarms' scenario.
insights.quote: Experiences including the IRA in Northern Ireland and the FARC in Colombia demonstrate that disarmament and state consolidation rarely succeed through coercion alone.
insights.locator: Experiences including the IRA in Northern Ireland

insights.claim: The author frames the outcome as binary and high-stakes: the current diplomatic process could either entrench both Hezbollah's armed status and a prolonged Israeli occupation for decades -- recreating conditions from 1982-2000 -- or produce Hezbollah's disarmament and restored Lebanese sovereignty.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: Defines the two tail scenarios for Lebanon risk over the coming months -- a repeat of the 18-year (1982-2000) occupation era is the explicit bear case if diplomacy fails.
insights.quote: could end up entrenching both Hezbollah’s armed status and a prolonged Israeli military occupation, possibly for decades. This would recreate the conditions that existed between 1982 and 2000.
insights.locator: The current diplomatic process could end up

insights.claim: Lebanon's government declared Hezbollah's and the IRGC's military activities illegal over the past year, which the author calls a historic turning point that must now be translated into actual expansion of state control starting in Beirut and the south.
insights.speaker: Paul Salem
insights.so_what: A concrete legal fact that provides domestic legal cover for LAF operations against Hezbollah assets -- worth tracking for enforcement follow-through, the real test of the policy's teeth.
insights.locator: The government’s decision over the past year
